summary: Cathy O'Neil comments on a Cuomo administration announcement about technological upgrades to NYC subways, including mobile tickets, wi-fi in stations, surveillance cameras on all trains and buses, and USB charging stations. She expresses skepticism about the priorities and longevity of these upgrades, suggesting that surveillance infrastructure will be maintained far better than amenities meant to serve the public.
key_points: NYC subways are getting mobile tickets, station wi-fi, surveillance cameras, and USB charging stations
The surveillance cameras will be on all subways and buses
Public amenities like USB chargers will likely fall into disrepair while surveillance infrastructure persists
There's an implicit critique of government priorities favoring monitoring over service
money_quotes: My guess: the surveillance cameras will continue to function long after the USB chargers get filled with gum.
key_insight: When governments invest in both public amenities and surveillance infrastructure, expect the surveillance to be maintained while the amenities decay.
